architect-manuscript.name: Architect the Manuscript
architect-manuscript.title: Architect the Manuscript
architect-manuscript.subtitle: A structural template for acts, scene dependencies, continuity risk, and the tools that keep the manuscript coherent.
architect-manuscript.scope: One manuscript where pacing, causality, and continuity need to be controlled explicitly.
architect-manuscript.objectiveRole: Make manuscript structure operational by linking acts, scene dependencies, continuity risks, and tooling.
architect-manuscript.templateObjective: Make the manuscript structurally defensible by surfacing major acts, scene dependencies, continuity risks, and the tools used to inspect them.
architect-manuscript.templateSuccessCondition: A reader can see what each act is doing, which scenes depend on earlier setup, and what continuity risks still threaten the manuscript.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.role: Summarize manuscript architecture pressure without carrying the structural evidence.
sections.rationale: This section compresses manuscript readiness while acts, dependencies, continuity risks, and tooling remain inspectable in their own layers.

sections.title: Act / Part Structure
sections.role: Hold the ordered act or part structure that defines the manuscript path.
sections.rationale: The manuscript needs an ordered outline before dependencies and continuity risks can be located meaningfully.

sections.title: Scene Dependency Map
sections.role: Map setup and payoff dependencies between scenes.
sections.rationale: Scene relationships need edge-table shape because causality lives between setup and dependent scenes rather than inside one card.

sections.title: Continuity Watchlist
sections.role: Track unresolved contradictions and continuity risks across scenes, characters, and storylines.
sections.rationale: Continuity risks must stay visible until checked because unresolved contradictions can invalidate manuscript structure.

sections.title: Tooling Surface
sections.role: Expose the tools used to inspect, verify, and evolve the manuscript structure.
sections.rationale: Structural and continuity claims need tool paths so the manuscript can be rechecked instead of reread from scratch.

relationships.rationale: Manuscript status should summarize the actual act structure being controlled.
Scene dependencies are meaningful only against the manuscript sequence and act structure they support.
Setup/payoff dependencies expose the continuity risks that need to remain visible until checked.
Continuity checks need an operational tool path so the watchlist can be verified or refreshed.

stageSignals.when: content-outline has no act or part cards
stageSignals.question: What first act, part, or structural unit makes this manuscript tangible?

stageSignals.when: act structure exists but scene-dependency-map has no dependency rows
stageSignals.question: Which scene setup or payoff dependency should be made explicit?

stageSignals.when: scene dependencies exist but continuity-watchlist has no risks or checks
stageSignals.question: Which continuity risk follows from the current scene dependencies?

stageSignals.when: act structure, dependencies, continuity, and tooling are all populated and linked
stageSignals.question: Is the manuscript structurally defensible, or does a dependency or continuity risk still control the next pass?

validOperations.label: Add act structure card
validOperations.description: Add an act, part, or structural unit with source references and notes.

validOperations.label: Add scene dependency
validOperations.description: Add a setup-to-dependent scene relationship that makes causality explicit.

validOperations.label: Add continuity risk
validOperations.description: Add a continuity risk tied to scenes, characters, or storylines.

validOperations.label: Link tooling to continuity
validOperations.description: Link a script, workflow, or artifact used to inspect manuscript structure or continuity.

competitor-watcher.name: Competitor Watcher
competitor-watcher.title: Competitor Watcher
competitor-watcher.subtitle: A living doc shape for tracking competitors across monitoring periods — strategic posture, discrete moves, indicators, and a market-position map. Each check-in lands as a delta, so the picture accumulates rather than restarts.
competitor-watcher.scope: One market, one set of named competitors, tracked over time. Strategic posture revealed through behavior. Discrete moves logged with intent and outcome. Indicators refreshed on a cadence. A two-axis map showing where each competitor sits and how they moved.
competitor-watcher.objectiveRole: Track competitive posture over time by separating competitors, moves, predictions, indicators, positions, sources, and period notes.
competitor-watcher.templateObjective: Track the evolution of <market>'s competitive landscape so any reader at any period check-in sees current strategic posture per competitor, the moves they made this period, live indicator values, predictions still pending, and where each player sits on the map vs. where they used to sit.
competitor-watcher.templateSuccessCondition: A reader opening the doc at the end of any monitoring period knows (1) each tracked competitor's current posture and how it shifted, (2) the discrete moves each one made in the period with intent and outcome, (3) the latest indicator values with dated sources, and (4) which competitors moved on the position map and in which direction.

sections.title: Tracked competitors
sections.role: Track named competitors and their current strategic posture.
sections.rationale: The named organizations whose strategic posture we watch over time. One card per competitor, with current bet and evolution surfaced as first-class fields.

sections.title: Strategic moves this period
sections.role: Log discrete competitor actions with intent and observed outcome.
sections.rationale: Discrete actions taken by tracked competitors with observable consequences. Moves are events; citations are what you read about them. Keep separate.

sections.title: What comes next
sections.role: Track predictions about competitor moves or market outcomes.
sections.rationale: Predictions about future competitor moves or market outcomes. Each rung resolves as evidence accumulates.

sections.title: Indicator dashboard
sections.role: Track numeric or categorical indicators that show competitive movement.
sections.rationale: Numeric indicators tracked across periods. Carry the value as text; carry direction as a categorical trend.

sections.title: Market position map
sections.role: Map competitor position and movement between periods.
sections.rationale: Two-axis placement of tracked competitors. Movement between periods is the primary signal; prior positions are preserved per card.

sections.title: Sources this period
sections.role: Preserve the sources consumed during the current competitive monitoring period.
sections.rationale: Articles, earnings transcripts, analyst reports, interviews you consumed this period. Sources are what you READ; moves are what HAPPENED. Keep them in their own sections.

sections.title: Period notes
sections.role: Summarize what moved, resolved, and matters for the next period.
sections.rationale: One card per monitoring period summarising what moved, what resolved, and what matters for the next period.

relationships.rationale: Competitor posture should be inferred from dated sources and observed behavior.
Strategic moves must identify which tracked competitor made the move.

evidence.description: A move should identify the competitor or company that made it.

relationships.rationale: Discrete moves should connect to indicator changes when their effect is observable.
Tracked competitors should appear on the market position map so movement is visible.
Indicator movement and strategic moves resolve or weaken predictions.
Period notes should summarize posture shifts, moves, indicators, predictions, and map movement.

stageSignals.when: competitor-stance-track has no tracked competitors
stageSignals.question: Which competitors define this market watch?

stageSignals.when: competitors exist but citation-feed has no sources
stageSignals.question: Which source supports the current competitor posture?

stageSignals.when: competitors exist but strategic-move-log lacks corresponding moves
stageSignals.question: Which tracked competitor made a discrete strategic move this period?

stageSignals.when: competitors exist but position-cluster-map is empty
stageSignals.question: Where does each competitor sit on the market position map this period?

stageSignals.when: competitors, moves, predictions, indicators, positions, sources, and notes are populated
stageSignals.question: Can a reader see each competitor posture, moves, indicators, and map movement for this period?

validOperations.label: Add competitor
validOperations.description: Add a tracked competitor with posture, current bet, and evolution.

validOperations.label: Add strategic move
validOperations.description: Add a dated strategic move by a tracked competitor.

validOperations.label: Record competitor indicator
validOperations.description: Add or update an indicator value connected to competitive movement.

validOperations.label: Update market position
validOperations.description: Add or update a competitor position and movement on the map.

validOperations.label: Write competitor period note
validOperations.description: Record what moved, resolved, and matters for next period.

design-system.name: Design a System
design-system.title: Design a System — your taste, one client at a time
design-system.subtitle: A small starting shape for designers building a client design system while keeping their own taste-signature visible and evidenced across prior work.
design-system.scope: The designer's recurring taste across prior systems, plus the system being built for the current client engagement — alignment to the brief, derivation moves from prior systems, and the current state of the new system as pointers.
design-system.objectiveRole: Make design-system work legible by connecting taste evidence, brief alignment, derivation moves, and surface pointers.
design-system.templateObjective: Make the design system you are building visible end-to-end: where each primitive lives, why it answers the brief, what it inherits from your prior work, and what about it is uniquely you.
design-system.templateSuccessCondition: Every primitive in the surface has a pointer (not a duplicated value). Every alignment card has both a constraint and a response. Every derivation card has a prior-system pointer and a one-or-two-sentence rationale. Every recurring taste card has at least two prior-system pointers as evidence.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.role: Summarize design-system readiness without carrying the design evidence.
sections.rationale: This section keeps client-system status visible while taste, alignment, derivation, and surface pointers stay distinct.

sections.title: Taste Signature
sections.role: Expose recurring designer traits evidenced by prior systems.
sections.rationale: Taste has to be evidenced through prior systems so it becomes an inspectable design input rather than a vague personal claim.

sections.title: Brief to System Alignment
sections.role: Translate client constraints into concrete system responses.
sections.rationale: Each alignment card pairs a brief constraint with a design response so the client system can be judged against the actual engagement.

sections.title: Design System Derivation
sections.role: Track what the new system inherits or transforms from prior work.
sections.rationale: Derivation cards keep inherited taste moves explicit and grounded in prior-system references.

sections.title: Design System Surface
sections.role: Expose the current design system as pointers to tokens, components, and motifs.
sections.rationale: The system surface should point to primitives where they live instead of duplicating token or component values inside the doc.

relationships.rationale: Design-system status should summarize the actual primitive surface being built.
Derivation moves should be grounded in evidenced taste from prior systems.

evidence.description: A derivation card should identify prior systems, tokens, components, or motifs that evidence the taste move.

relationships.rationale: System primitives should be traceable to brief constraints and responses.
Derivation moves become useful when they point at actual tokens, components, or motifs in the system surface.

stageSignals.when: design-system-surface has no primitive pointers
stageSignals.question: What first token, component, or motif pointer makes this client system tangible?

stageSignals.when: taste-signature exists but design-system-derivation has no corresponding derivation moves
stageSignals.question: Which taste trait still needs a derivation move into the client system?

stageSignals.when: system surface exists but brief-to-system-alignment has no cards
stageSignals.question: Which brief constraint does the current primitive answer?

stageSignals.when: taste, alignment, derivation, and surface are all populated and linked
stageSignals.question: Can the system be defended as client-aligned, pointer-based, and distinctively grounded in prior taste?

validOperations.label: Add system surface primitive
validOperations.description: Add a token, component, or motif pointer that belongs to the client system.

validOperations.label: Add taste trait
validOperations.description: Add a taste-signature trait evidenced by prior systems.

validOperations.label: Add brief alignment
validOperations.description: Add a constraint/response card that ties the system to the client brief.

validOperations.label: Add derivation card
validOperations.description: Add a derivation move from prior system evidence to a current primitive.

map-themes-storylines.name: Map Themes and Storylines
map-themes-storylines.title: Map Themes and Storylines
map-themes-storylines.subtitle: A narrative mapping shape for when the manuscript exists enough that themes, arcs, and characters need their own structure.
map-themes-storylines.scope: One manuscript where themes, storylines, and character carriers need to be tracked together.
map-themes-storylines.objectiveRole: Map narrative meaning by linking themes, storylines, characters, and manuscript pressure.
map-themes-storylines.templateObjective: Make the manuscript's meaning threads explicit so a reader can see which themes, storylines, and characters are actually carrying the book.
map-themes-storylines.templateSuccessCondition: A reader can identify the major themes, how each storyline moves them, which characters carry the pressure, and where the manuscript is thin or overloaded.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.role: Summarize narrative-thread health without carrying the thread evidence.
sections.rationale: This section compresses thread health while themes, storylines, and character carriers remain distinct.

sections.title: Theme Thread Map
sections.role: Track deliberate themes and the story material carrying them.
sections.rationale: Themes need their own thread map so meaning pressure can be traced through storylines, characters, and scenes.

sections.title: Storyline Arc Map
sections.role: Track named arcs, trajectories, beats, and open loops.
sections.rationale: Storylines show how themes move over time and where arcs are thin, overloaded, or unresolved.

sections.title: Character Surface
sections.role: Track which characters carry theme and storyline pressure.
sections.rationale: Characters are structural carriers of theme and arc pressure, so their role must remain visible.

relationships.rationale: Narrative status should summarize the actual theme thread map.
Themes become manuscript structure when storylines carry them through arcs and beats.

evidence.description: A storyline should identify the theme, scene, or source material it carries.

relationships.rationale: Storylines become concrete when characters carry their pressure and continuity concerns.

evidence.description: A character should identify the storyline, scene, or source material it carries.

stageSignals.when: theme-thread-map has no theme cards
stageSignals.question: What first theme thread makes the manuscript meaning tangible?

stageSignals.when: themes exist but storyline-arc-map lacks corresponding arcs
stageSignals.question: Which theme needs a storyline arc that carries it?

stageSignals.when: storylines exist but character-surface has no carriers
stageSignals.question: Which character carries the current storyline pressure?

stageSignals.when: themes, storylines, and characters are populated and linked
stageSignals.question: Can a reader see which themes, storylines, and characters are carrying the book?

validOperations.label: Add theme thread
validOperations.description: Add a theme with articulation, why it matters, and pressure points.

validOperations.label: Add storyline arc
validOperations.description: Add a storyline arc that carries a theme through beats and scenes.

validOperations.label: Add character surface
validOperations.description: Add a character card that carries storyline and theme pressure.

monitoring-tracker.name: Monitoring Tracker
monitoring-tracker.title: Monitoring Tracker
monitoring-tracker.subtitle: A living doc shape for topics that don't end — track named voices, empirical predictions, and numeric indicators every check-in, with each period visible as a delta.
monitoring-tracker.scope: One topic or domain, tracked over time. Named experts whose stances evolve; predictions whose resolution accumulates evidence; indicators whose values print on a cadence; a consensus map where movement between periods is the signal.
monitoring-tracker.objectiveRole: Track an evolving topic across periods by separating voices, predictions, indicators, map movement, sources, and period notes.
monitoring-tracker.templateObjective: Track the evolution of <topic> as evidence accumulates and named positions shift, so any reader at any period check-in sees current stance, live prediction resolutions, latest indicator values, and what moved since the prior period — without re-reading the underlying sources.
monitoring-tracker.templateSuccessCondition: A reader opening the doc at the end of any monitoring period knows (1) each expert's current stance and how it shifted, (2) which predictions moved toward resolution, (3) the latest indicator values with dated sources, and (4) where the consensus has tightened or widened on the map.

sections.title: Tracked voices
sections.role: Track named voices and how their stances evolve over monitoring periods.
sections.rationale: The named voices whose public-intellectual stance evolves over time. One card per voice.

sections.title: Empirical predictions
sections.role: Track what-to-watch claims as evidence accumulates.
sections.rationale: The 'what to watch' claims rendered as a proof ladder. Each rung resolves as evidence accumulates across periods.

sections.title: Indicator dashboard
sections.role: Track numeric indicators and their direction across periods.
sections.rationale: Numeric indicators tracked across periods. Latest value is text; trend is categorical (rising/stable/falling/resolved).

sections.title: Consensus & divergence map
sections.role: Map current and prior positions so movement between periods is visible.
sections.rationale: Two-axis placement of tracked voices. Movement between monitoring periods is the primary signal. Prior-period position is preserved on each card.

sections.title: Sources this period
sections.role: Preserve the dated sources consumed during the current monitoring period.
sections.rationale: Period-grouped citation feed. Each source added in the current period lives here; retroactive additions to prior periods are allowed but should be flagged in notes.

sections.title: Period notes
sections.role: Summarize what shifted, resolved, and matters for the next period.
sections.rationale: One card per monitoring period summarising what shifted, what resolved, and what matters for next.

relationships.rationale: Expert stance updates should be grounded in sources read during the monitoring period.
Indicator values need dated source backing so period movement can be audited.
The consensus map should place tracked voices and preserve their movement between periods.
Predictions move toward resolution when indicators change or new values arrive.
Period notes should summarize stance shifts, prediction movement, indicator changes, and map movement.

stageSignals.when: expert-stance-track has no tracked voices
stageSignals.question: Which named voices define this monitoring topic?

stageSignals.when: expert stances exist but citation-feed has no sources
stageSignals.question: Which dated source supports the current stance update?

stageSignals.when: expert stances exist but position-cluster-map is empty
stageSignals.question: Where do the tracked voices sit on the consensus map this period?

stageSignals.when: indicators exist but proof-ladder has no prediction rungs
stageSignals.question: Which prediction did the indicator value move toward resolution?

stageSignals.when: voices, predictions, indicators, map, sources, and period notes are populated
stageSignals.question: Can a reader understand what shifted this period without rereading the sources?

validOperations.label: Add tracked voice
validOperations.description: Add a named expert or voice with current stance and evolution.

validOperations.label: Add period source
validOperations.description: Add a dated source consumed during the current monitoring period.

validOperations.label: Update position map
validOperations.description: Add or update the consensus/divergence map position for a tracked voice.

validOperations.label: Record indicator value
validOperations.description: Add or update a dated numeric indicator value and trend.

validOperations.label: Write period note
validOperations.description: Record what shifted, resolved, and matters for the next monitoring period.

operations-support.name: Operations and Support
operations-support.title: Operations and Support
operations-support.subtitle: A compact starting shape for mediated workflows, support surfaces, and the tools that keep them operational.
operations-support.scope: A support or operations domain where requests, operational surfaces, enablers, and tooling need to be reasoned about together.
operations-support.objectiveRole: Make an operations or support domain legible across request flow, operating surfaces, enabling assets, and tooling.
operations-support.templateObjective: Make an operations or support domain legible across request flow, operational surfaces, enabling assets, and the tools used to support the work.
operations-support.templateSuccessCondition: A reader can see how requests enter the system, which support paths exist, what enablers are available, and which tools the operators rely on.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.role: Summarize operational readiness and current support pressure without carrying detailed request or tooling evidence.
sections.rationale: This section keeps operational readiness visible without turning requests, support lanes, enablers, or tooling into dashboard prose.

sections.title: Mediated Operation
sections.role: Describe the mediated request-to-execution flow that operators need to run.
sections.rationale: The core operation has to name how work enters, moves, and gets mediated before support surfaces can be evaluated.

sections.title: Operating Surface
sections.role: Expose the bounded support lanes or operational paths that request flow routes into.
sections.rationale: Support lanes are separated from request flow so each bounded operating path can be assessed and improved.

sections.title: Enabler Catalog
sections.role: Catalog compact support assets that improve operator speed, reliability, or coverage.
sections.rationale: Operators need visible snippets, assets, fixtures, or runbooks that make the operating surfaces easier and safer to run.

sections.title: Tooling Surface
sections.role: Expose the scripts, workflows, and local tools used to operate or evolve the support domain.
sections.rationale: Operational claims need concrete tool paths so support work can be rerun, inspected, or extended.

relationships.rationale: Operational status should summarize real support flow rather than stand apart as a dashboard label.
Mediated operations should name the concrete support lanes or operating surfaces they route requests into.

evidence.description: An operating surface should identify the operation, workflow path, or request input it supports.

relationships.rationale: Support lanes stay operational only when their enabling snippets, fixtures, runbooks, or assets are visible.
Operational request flow needs a tool path so a future session can rerun, inspect, or extend the support process.

stageSignals.when: operation has no request-flow cards
stageSignals.question: What first real request flow, intake path, or mediated operation makes this support doc tangible?

stageSignals.when: operation exists but operating-surface has no corresponding support lanes
stageSignals.question: Which operation cards lack a concrete support lane or operating surface?

stageSignals.when: operating-surface has lanes but enabler-catalog has no assets that support them
stageSignals.question: Which enabler would make the current support lane faster, safer, or easier to operate?

stageSignals.when: operation, operating surface, enablers, and tooling are all populated and linked
stageSignals.question: Can the support domain be operated from this document, or are key request paths still implicit?

validOperations.label: Add operation card
validOperations.description: Add a request-flow or mediated-operation card that names how work enters and moves through the support domain.

validOperations.label: Add operating surface card
validOperations.description: Add a bounded support lane or operational surface that a request flow routes into.

validOperations.label: Add enabler card
validOperations.description: Add a support asset, runbook, fixture, or snippet that strengthens an operating surface.

validOperations.label: Link tooling to operation
validOperations.description: Link the script, workflow, or local tool used to run or inspect the operation.

validOperations.label: Lower support status
validOperations.description: Lower or qualify operational readiness when support lanes, enablers, or tooling do not support the claim.

oss-issue-deep-dive.name: OSS Issue Deep-Dive
oss-issue-deep-dive.title: OSS Issue Deep-Dive
oss-issue-deep-dive.subtitle: A living doc for one stuck issue in an open-source repo — ready to load into a Claude Code or Codex session and keep updating as work progresses.
oss-issue-deep-dive.scope: One GitHub issue in an external OSS repo and its orbiting archaeology (related issues, PRs, code, maintainer stances).
oss-issue-deep-dive.objectiveRole: Make one stuck open-source issue operational by preserving symptoms, source anchors, attempts, related issue orbit, and maintainer stances as a handoff surface.
oss-issue-deep-dive.templateObjective: Replace with the objective of the focal issue — the one sentence that captures what would be different after the fix.
oss-issue-deep-dive.templateSuccessCondition: A fresh agent (or human) can load this doc into a session and start engineering work on the issue without walking the archaeology again.

sections.title: Symptoms
sections.role: Hold reproducible observed behavior with environment, witness, and repro steps.
sections.rationale: Reproducible behaviours with attributable witnesses. Every card needs repro steps and a named observer — that is what separates a symptom from a generic finding.

sections.title: Findings
sections.role: Record synthesized facts learned during investigation that are not themselves repro steps.
sections.rationale: Synthesized claims that are not specific reproducible observations — e.g. 'the ANSI codes are idempotent' or 'the framework re-initializes on focus change'. If you find yourself writing a repro path, move the card to symptoms. Delete this section if every observation is repro-backed.

sections.title: Code anchors
sections.role: Pin the code locations, revisions, and mechanics that can plausibly explain or repair the issue.
sections.rationale: Revision-pinned pointers into the source. A reader or agent can jump straight to the file and line — no regrep required. The pinned revision makes drift visible on re-crystallize.

sections.title: Attempts
sections.role: Track probes, rejected fixes, shipped workarounds, and what each attempt proved.
sections.rationale: Every action tried against the problem and what it proved. Probes get marked superseded when a shipped attempt productionizes the same insight. Rejected attempts stay — they tell the next reader what not to try.

sections.title: Issue orbit
sections.role: Map sibling issues and PRs that share root cause, constraints, or prior resolution evidence.
sections.rationale: Graph of sibling issues and PRs with an explicit relationship to the focal one. If the relationship is weak (only topical overlap), drop the card — orbit is for structural relationships, not 'this also mentions mouse'.

sections.title: Stances
sections.role: Preserve stakeholder positions that constrain what fix is acceptable or what uncertainty remains.
sections.rationale: Named positions from stakeholders in the thread — maintainer, contributor, reporter. Stances are not decisions; they evolve. Preserve the original text so the next contributor doesn't re-litigate.

relationships.rationale: A deep-dive is not actionable until observed symptoms are tied to revision-pinned code mechanics.

evidence.description: A code anchor should identify the symptom card it explains, bounds, or makes inspectable.

relationships.rationale: Anchors become useful when an attempt probes, modifies, or rules out the mechanics they identify.

evidence.description: An attempt should name the anchor, code path, or touched path it is testing.

relationships.rationale: Attempt outcomes should be captured as findings before they become final recommendations or future work.
Related issues and PRs should explain whether the symptom is isolated, recurring, duplicated, or already partly fixed.
Maintainer and contributor positions should shape which repair attempts are acceptable, blocked, or worth avoiding.

stageSignals.when: symptom-observation has no repro-backed symptom cards
stageSignals.question: What observed behavior, witness, environment, and repro path make this issue tangible?

stageSignals.when: symptoms exist but code-anchor has no corresponding mechanics
stageSignals.question: Which symptom lacks a revision-pinned code anchor or mechanical explanation?

stageSignals.when: code anchors exist but attempt-log has no probes against them
stageSignals.question: Which code anchor still needs a probe, rejected attempt, or shipped workaround?

stageSignals.when: issue orbit or maintainer stance may have shifted since the last deep-dive pass
stageSignals.question: What changed in related issues, PRs, or stakeholder positions since this doc was last updated?

stageSignals.when: symptoms, anchors, attempts, issue orbit, and maintainer stances are populated and linked
stageSignals.question: Can a fresh contributor start engineering work from this doc without repeating the issue archaeology?

validOperations.label: Add symptom
validOperations.description: Add a repro-backed symptom card with environment, witness, and reproduction steps.

validOperations.label: Add code anchor
validOperations.description: Add a revision-pinned code anchor that explains, bounds, or makes a symptom inspectable.

validOperations.label: Add attempt
validOperations.description: Add a probe, rejected fix, or shipped workaround against a code anchor.

validOperations.label: Record finding
validOperations.description: Record the factual finding produced by an attempt before using it as handoff guidance.

validOperations.label: Refresh issue orbit
validOperations.description: Refresh related issues, PRs, and maintainer stances before treating the handoff as current.

proof-canonicality.name: Proof and Canonicality
proof-canonicality.title: Proof and Canonicality
proof-canonicality.subtitle: A starting shape for model, parser, protocol, or truth-claim work where assertions, findings, and proof strength matter.
proof-canonicality.scope: A domain where formal claims, staged proof, findings, and decisions need to be kept distinct but connected.
proof-canonicality.objectiveRole: Make a truth-bearing domain legible by separating model, assertion, proof, findings, decisions, and tooling.
proof-canonicality.templateObjective: Make a truth-bearing domain legible by separating the model, the assertion, the proof ladder, the findings, the decisions, and the tooling.
proof-canonicality.templateSuccessCondition: A reader can tell what is being claimed, what proof exists, where evidence is weak, what was learned, and which tools are used to evolve or verify the claim.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.role: Summarize proof posture and canonicality pressure without collapsing the evidence layers.
sections.rationale: This section keeps proof posture visible while leaving claims, evidence, findings, decisions, and tooling in their own accountable layers.

sections.title: Formal Model
sections.role: Define the primitives, invariants, authorities, and scope that make the domain truth-bearing.
sections.rationale: A truth-bearing domain needs explicit primitives, invariants, authorities, and scope before assertions can be evaluated.

sections.title: Model Assertion
sections.role: State the claims that the proof structure is trying to support or weaken.
sections.rationale: Assertions are kept separate from the model and proof so claim strength can change without rewriting the underlying domain.

sections.title: Proof Ladder
sections.role: Track staged evidence rungs that increase confidence in the assertions.
sections.rationale: Proof strength needs staged rungs so weak evidence, partial proof, and canonical proof do not collapse into one status.

sections.title: Investigation Findings
sections.role: Record observed facts learned from proof work without turning them directly into decisions.
sections.rationale: Findings capture what proof work established before those observations are promoted into decisions.

sections.title: Decision Record
sections.role: Preserve canonical choices justified by the model, proof, and findings.
sections.rationale: Decisions belong in their own section because canonical choices must be justified by the model, proof ladder, and findings.

sections.title: Tooling Surface
sections.role: Expose the scripts, skills, and workflows used to inspect, prove, or evolve the domain.
sections.rationale: Proof claims need operational tool paths so evidence can be rerun, challenged, or extended.

relationships.rationale: Assertions are only meaningful inside the model primitives, invariants, and authority scope they claim against.
A model assertion should not advance toward canonicality without staged proof strength.

evidence.description: A proof rung should identify the assertion, primitive, invariant, or authority it supports or challenges.

relationships.rationale: Proof work should produce factual findings before those findings become operating decisions.
Decisions should be justified by observed findings, not by unsupported assertions.
Proof rungs need an operational tool path so the evidence can be rerun or extended.

stageSignals.when: formal-model has no source-backed cards
stageSignals.question: What first model primitive, invariant, or authority makes this proof domain tangible?

stageSignals.when: model-assertion exists but proof-ladder has no corresponding proof rungs
stageSignals.question: Which assertion lacks proof support, or which proof rung is attached to the wrong claim?

stageSignals.when: proof-ladder has rungs but investigation-findings has no observed findings
stageSignals.question: What finding did the current proof rung actually establish?

stageSignals.when: model, assertion, proof, findings, decisions, and tooling are all populated and linked
stageSignals.question: What claim can responsibly become canonical, and what uncertainty remains?

validOperations.label: Add model primitive
validOperations.description: Add a formal model card that names the primitive, invariant, authority, or scope being reasoned over.

validOperations.label: Add proof rung
validOperations.description: Add a proof rung that directly supports or challenges a model assertion.

validOperations.label: Record investigation finding
validOperations.description: Record the factual finding produced by proof work before deciding what it means.

validOperations.label: Weaken assertion
validOperations.description: Lower or qualify an assertion when proof strength does not support canonical status.

validOperations.label: Record canonical decision
validOperations.description: Record a decision justified by the model, proof ladder, and findings.

starter-prove-claim.name: Prove a Claim
starter-prove-claim.title: Prove a Claim
starter-prove-claim.subtitle: A small starting shape for work where a single assertion and the strength of its proof need to stay explicit.
starter-prove-claim.scope: One truth claim, model claim, or parser claim that needs to be defended with staged evidence.
starter-prove-claim.objectiveRole: Seed a proof doc by separating the claim from its proof ladder.
starter-prove-claim.templateObjective: Make the main claim and its current proof strength visible without collapsing claim, evidence, and conclusions together.
starter-prove-claim.templateSuccessCondition: A reader can say what is being claimed, what evidence currently supports it, and what stronger proof would look like next.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.role: Summarize proof posture without carrying the claim or evidence itself.
sections.rationale: This section keeps claim readiness visible while the assertion and proof ladder remain distinct.

sections.title: Model Assertion
sections.role: State the claim being defended or weakened.
sections.rationale: The claim needs its own card so proof strength can change without rewriting what is being claimed.

sections.title: Proof Ladder
sections.role: Track the evidence rungs that support or weaken the assertion.
sections.rationale: Proof strength needs staged rungs so current evidence and next proof are visible separately from the assertion.

relationships.rationale: Proof status should summarize the actual claim under review.
A claim cannot be treated as operational truth without staged proof strength.

evidence.description: A proof rung should identify the claim, primitive, invariant, or authority it supports or challenges.

stageSignals.when: model-assertion has no claim card
stageSignals.question: What exact claim should this proof doc defend or weaken?

stageSignals.when: model-assertion exists but proof-ladder has no corresponding proof rungs
stageSignals.question: Which proof rung directly supports or challenges the claim?

stageSignals.when: assertion and proof ladder are populated and linked
stageSignals.question: What stronger proof would move this claim beyond its current status?

validOperations.label: Add assertion
validOperations.description: Add the claim being defended, with primitives, invariants, or authorities if known.

validOperations.label: Add proof rung
validOperations.description: Add evidence that supports, weakens, or scopes the assertion.

starter-run-support-ops.name: Run Support Ops
starter-run-support-ops.title: Run Support Ops
starter-run-support-ops.subtitle: A small starting shape for support work where request flow and operator tooling matter first.
starter-run-support-ops.scope: One support or operations domain where requests enter through a mediated path and operators need a clear tool layer.
starter-run-support-ops.objectiveRole: Seed an operations doc with request flow and the operator tooling that supports it.
starter-run-support-ops.templateObjective: Make the request path and operator tool layer legible enough that someone can quickly understand how the support domain currently runs.
starter-run-support-ops.templateSuccessCondition: A reader can see how a request enters the system, who or what fulfills it, and which tools the operators trust.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.role: Summarize support readiness without carrying request-flow details.
sections.rationale: This section keeps operational status visible while request flow and tool support remain inspectable.

sections.title: Mediated Operation
sections.role: Describe how requests enter and move through the support domain.
sections.rationale: The mediated operation is the core of the support domain because it names how work enters, is fulfilled, and moves next.

sections.title: Tooling Surface
sections.role: Expose trusted tools used by operators to run or inspect the request flow.
sections.rationale: Operators need visible tooling so the support flow can be run without rediscovering scripts or workflows.

relationships.rationale: Support status should summarize the actual mediated operation.
A request flow is operational only when operators can see the trusted tools that support it.

stageSignals.when: operation has no request-flow cards
stageSignals.question: What first request path or mediated operation makes this support domain tangible?

stageSignals.when: operation exists but tooling-surface has no trusted tool card
stageSignals.question: Which tool does the operator trust to run, inspect, or update this support flow?

stageSignals.when: operation and tooling are populated
stageSignals.question: Can a reader understand how requests enter and which tools operators trust?

validOperations.label: Add operation card
validOperations.description: Add a request-flow card naming the intake path, current support, and next step.

validOperations.label: Add operator tooling
validOperations.description: Add the trusted script, workflow, or tool used by operators.

starter-ship-feature.name: Ship a Feature
starter-ship-feature.title: Ship a Feature
starter-ship-feature.subtitle: A small starting shape for one feature that needs intent, delivery focus, and a tool layer.
starter-ship-feature.scope: One feature or product surface that needs to move from idea into working behavior.
starter-ship-feature.objectiveRole: Seed a feature delivery doc with a primary surface and the tooling needed to work on it.
starter-ship-feature.templateObjective: Make the main feature legible enough that a teammate can see what it is, what matters most, and how to work on it.
starter-ship-feature.templateSuccessCondition: A reader can name the primary surface, the current delivery target, and the tools used to update or render the doc.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.role: Summarize feature delivery readiness without replacing the surface or tooling cards.
sections.rationale: This section gives a small feature doc a quick readiness signal while the actual feature and tool evidence stays separate.

sections.title: Design–Code–Spec Flow
sections.role: Name the primary feature surface as a joined design, code, spec, interaction, and ticket entity.
sections.rationale: A feature needs one concrete surface card before delivery work can be understood or handed off.

sections.title: Tooling Surface
sections.role: Expose the scripts, workflows, and artifacts used to update or render the feature doc.
sections.rationale: A teammate needs an operational tool path to keep the feature doc current instead of only reading the feature card.

relationships.rationale: Feature status should summarize the real surface being shipped.
The feature surface is operational only when the update/render tools are visible.

stageSignals.when: design-code-spec-flow has no feature card
stageSignals.question: What primary feature surface should this starter doc make tangible first?

stageSignals.when: feature surface exists but tooling-surface has no tool path
stageSignals.question: Which script, workflow, or artifact lets a teammate update or render this feature doc?

stageSignals.when: feature surface and tooling are populated
stageSignals.question: Can a teammate name the feature, current delivery target, and tools from this doc?

validOperations.label: Add feature surface
validOperations.description: Add the primary design-code-spec feature card.

validOperations.label: Add feature tooling
validOperations.description: Add a tool card that explains how to update, render, or inspect the feature doc.

starter-tiny-experiment.name: Run Tiny Experiments
starter-tiny-experiment.title: Run Tiny Experiments
starter-tiny-experiment.subtitle: A small starting shape for work where personal, time-bounded experiments need an explicit hypothesis, window, and persist/pause/pivot closure.
starter-tiny-experiment.scope: A small set of running and recently-closed pacts, treated as the unit of learning rather than the unit of finishing.
starter-tiny-experiment.objectiveRole: Make time-bounded pacts operational by separating hypotheses, signals, attempts, and closure decisions.
starter-tiny-experiment.templateObjective: Make the experiments you are actually running visible — their hypotheses, their windows, and the closure decision waiting at the end of each.
starter-tiny-experiment.templateSuccessCondition: Every running pact has an end date and at least one trackable signal. Every closed pact has an outcome (persist / pause / pivot) and a one-or-two-sentence rationale.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.role: Summarize experiment pressure without carrying pact details.
sections.rationale: This section keeps current experiment pressure visible while pact hypotheses and attempts stay inspectable.

sections.title: Tiny Experiments
sections.role: Track each pact with hypothesis, window, signals, and closure state.
sections.rationale: Each experiment is a pact: hypothesis, time window, signals, and closure decision are the unit of learning.

sections.title: Attempts
sections.role: Record actions tried during or around the experiments and what they proved.
sections.rationale: Attempts preserve what was tried so pact outcomes are grounded in behavior rather than memory.

relationships.rationale: Experiment status should summarize actual running or recently closed pacts.
Pact closure should be informed by what was tried and what it proved.

stageSignals.when: tiny-experiment has no pact cards
stageSignals.question: What first pact, hypothesis, window, and signal should be tracked?

stageSignals.when: tiny experiments exist but attempt-log has no attempts
stageSignals.question: What action has been tried, and what did it prove for the pact?

stageSignals.when: experiments and attempts are populated
stageSignals.question: Which pacts should persist, pause, or pivot based on the tracked signals?

validOperations.label: Add experiment pact
validOperations.description: Add a tiny experiment with hypothesis, window, and signals.

validOperations.label: Record experiment attempt
validOperations.description: Record what was tried and what it proved for the pact.

validOperations.label: Close experiment pact
validOperations.description: Set an outcome and rationale for a pact at the end of its window.

starter-write-book.name: Write a Book
starter-write-book.title: Write a Book
starter-write-book.subtitle: A small starting shape for drafting a book without losing chapter focus, momentum, and the tool layer.
starter-write-book.scope: One manuscript or book project moving from outline into drafted chapters.
starter-write-book.objectiveRole: Seed a book drafting doc with chapter production state and tooling.
starter-write-book.templateObjective: Make the manuscript legible enough that someone can see what the book is, which chapters are moving, and how the work is being supported.
starter-write-book.templateSuccessCondition: A reader can identify the current chapter set, the state of each major drafting unit, and the tools used to keep the manuscript moving.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.role: Summarize manuscript drafting momentum without carrying chapter details.
sections.rationale: This section keeps drafting status visible while chapters and tooling remain inspectable.

sections.title: Chapter Flow
sections.role: Track major chapter or drafting units as production cards.
sections.rationale: A book draft needs chapter-level production cards so momentum and dependencies are visible.

sections.title: Tooling Surface
sections.role: Expose tools used to keep drafting work moving.
sections.rationale: Drafting claims need an operational tool layer so chapter state can be updated and inspected.

relationships.rationale: Book status should summarize the current chapter production state.
Chapter production needs tools for drafting, tracking, or rendering the manuscript.

stageSignals.when: content-production has no chapter cards
stageSignals.question: What first chapter or drafting unit makes this book project tangible?

stageSignals.when: chapter flow exists but tooling-surface has no tool cards
stageSignals.question: Which tool keeps the chapter work moving or inspectable?

stageSignals.when: chapter flow and tooling are populated
stageSignals.question: Can a reader identify the chapter set, their state, and the tools supporting the draft?

validOperations.label: Add chapter card
validOperations.description: Add a chapter or drafting unit with synopsis, target, and dependencies.

validOperations.label: Add drafting tooling
validOperations.description: Add a tool used to draft, render, or inspect manuscript progress.

surface-delivery.name: Surface Delivery
surface-delivery.title: Surface Delivery
surface-delivery.subtitle: A compact starting shape for product surfaces that need design, implementation, verification, and tooling in one place.
surface-delivery.scope: A feature or product surface moving from design intent through implementation and verification.
surface-delivery.objectiveRole: Make one product surface legible across intent, implementation state, verification readiness, and tooling.
surface-delivery.templateObjective: Make one product surface legible across intent, implementation state, verification readiness, and the tools used to work on it.
surface-delivery.templateSuccessCondition: A reader can identify the main surface, current implementation drift, immediate verification gaps, and which tools are used to evolve the work.

sections.title: Status Snapshot
sections.role: Summarize delivery pressure and current readiness without carrying the detailed evidence.
sections.rationale: This section keeps delivery readiness visible without mixing summary state into the design, alignment, verification, or tooling evidence.

sections.title: Design–Code–Spec Flow
sections.role: Hold the product surface as a joined design, code, spec, interaction, and ticket entity.
sections.rationale: The product surface needs one joined entity that can name design intent, implementation paths, specs, interactions, and tickets together.

sections.title: Design–Implementation Alignment
sections.role: Judge whether implementation targets satisfy the design and spec intent carried by the surface flow.
sections.rationale: The surface-flow cards need explicit implementation judgments so visual or code presence is not mistaken for delivery alignment.

sections.title: Verification Checkpoints
sections.role: Track the checks that prove the aligned implementation is ready enough to ship or continue.
sections.rationale: Alignment claims need concrete checks that can be rerun or extended before readiness is treated as real.

sections.title: Tooling Surface
sections.role: Expose the scripts, workflows, and local tools used to inspect, evolve, and verify the surface.
sections.rationale: Verification and future updates need visible tool paths so the surface can be inspected without rediscovering the workflow.

relationships.rationale: Status is only meaningful when it summarizes the actual surface flow being delivered.
Alignment rows should judge the design/code/spec entities named in the surface flow.

evidence.description: An alignment row should identify the surface-flow card, Figma node, or code path it judges.

relationships.rationale: Implementation alignment is not delivery readiness until checks exist for the aligned behavior.
Verification checkpoints need an operational tool path so a future session can rerun or extend them.

stageSignals.when: design-code-spec-flow has no source-backed cards
stageSignals.question: What first real surface, spec, code path, and ticket make this delivery doc tangible?

stageSignals.when: design-code-spec-flow exists but design-implementation-alignment has no corresponding rows
stageSignals.question: Which surface-flow cards lack an implementation-alignment judgment?

stageSignals.when: alignment rows are partial/current but verification-checkpoints do not cover the same surface concerns
stageSignals.question: Which verification checkpoint would make the current implementation alignment actionable?

stageSignals.when: status-snapshot or alignment state is older than source/code/check updates
stageSignals.question: What changed since the last delivery pass, and which status should move?

stageSignals.when: surface flow, alignment, verification, and tooling are all populated and linked
stageSignals.question: Is the surface shippable, blocked, or only visually/partially complete?

validOperations.label: Add surface flow card
validOperations.description: Add the first source-backed surface card with design/spec/code/ticket references.

validOperations.label: Add alignment row
validOperations.description: Add or update an alignment row for a surface-flow entity that lacks implementation judgment.

validOperations.label: Add verification checkpoint
validOperations.description: Add a check that proves or bounds a current alignment claim.

validOperations.label: Lower readiness status
validOperations.description: Lower or qualify status when verification does not support the delivery claim.

validOperations.label: Link tooling to check
validOperations.description: Link the script, workflow, or local tool that can run or inspect a verification checkpoint.
